symptom: SSL grading sites (e.g. SSL Labs) flag Application Gateway for not supporting Forward Secrecy or Perfect Forward Secrecy, resulting in a security penalty rating
rootCause: Application Gateway default SSL/TLS policy prioritizes backward compatibility and includes cipher suites that do not use forward secrecy key exchange algorithms
solution: Configure a newer predefined SSL policy or a custom policy with forward-secrecy-capable cipher suites: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/application-gateway/application-gateway-ssl-policy-overview
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Expert Troubleshooting/Handling "Penalty for not using forward secrecy (B)" cases

symptom: ExpressRoute Private Peering DSCP QoS values are reset to 0 when packets hit the MSEEs - customer configured DSCP markings are not preserved across ExpressRoute Private Peering circuit
rootCause: Azure ExpressRoute Private Peering circuits do not support QoS queues. DSCP values configured on the customer/provider side get reset to 0 at the MSEEs. Only Microsoft Peering supports QoS.
solution: Inform customer that QoS queues for Private Peering are not supported. DSCP values will be reset to 0 on Private Peering. QoS is only supported on Microsoft Peering.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExR Bursting Capabilities & Considerations

symptom: Customer attempts to use Layer-3 router redundancy protocols (GLBP, HSRP, or VRRP) with ExpressRoute circuits but they are not functioning
rootCause: Azure ExpressRoute does not support Layer-3 redundancy protocols such as GLBP, HSRP, or VRRP on the MSEE side.
solution: Inform customer that Layer-3 protocols (GLBP, HSRP, VRRP) are not supported with ExpressRoute. Use ECMP with dual Active/Active CE/PE links and BGP for redundancy instead.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExR Bursting Capabilities & Considerations

symptom: ExpressRoute Gateway selects sub-optimal path when different weight values are applied to connections with Global Reach enabled circuits - traffic goes through transit path instead of direct path
rootCause: When different weight values are applied to ExpressRoute connections, weight attribute has highest BGP priority, overriding the AS_PATH prepend mechanism (community 12076:12077) used by Global Reach for best path selection. This causes ERGW to prefer the transit (longer) path through Global Reach.
solution: Do not apply different weight values to ExpressRoute connections when Global Reach is enabled between circuits connected to the same gateway. ERGW uses AS_PATH prepend (community 12076:12077) for best path selection with Global Reach - weight overrides this mechanism.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/Express Route Global Reach Advance

symptom: On-premises network connected to ExpressRoute circuit X cannot reach on-premises network connected to circuit Z, even though both circuits have Global Reach enabled with an intermediate circuit Y (X-Y and Y-Z)
rootCause: MSEEs use iBGP between Global Reach connected circuits. BGP rule prevents routes learned from iBGP neighbors from being re-advertised to other iBGP neighbors, blocking transitive routing through intermediate circuits.
solution: Enable Global Reach directly between circuit X and circuit Z. ExpressRoute Global Reach does not support transitive routing - each pair of on-premises networks must have their corresponding circuits explicitly connected via Global Reach.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/Express Route Global Reach Advance

symptom: ExpressRoute Gateway creation fails with error: ExpressRoute Gateway customers will no longer need to create a Public IP. Microsoft will now auto-create and manage the Public IP - when using old PowerShell/CLI commands
rootCause: New ExpressRoute Gateway deployment model auto-creates and manages public IPs in Gateway Tenant subscriptions with zone-redundant configuration. Old commands that manually create/associate public IPs are no longer supported for new or upgraded gateways.
solution: Use updated PowerShell/CLI commands that do not specify a public IP address. The gateway will auto-create a zone-redundant public IP. The public endpoint is now hidden from the end user.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Auto-assigned Public IP

symptom: Gateway SKU Migration blade is not visible in Azure portal for a previously migrated ExpressRoute gateway that needs re-migration
rootCause: When gateways are migrated, a tag CreatedBy:GatewaySKUMigration is added. Gateways with this tag will not display the Gateway SKU Migration blade in the portal.
solution: Remove the CreatedBy:GatewaySKUMigration tag from the gateway resource to access the Gateway SKU Migration blade again. Re-running migration will also update the public IP to auto-assigned.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Auto-assigned Public IP

symptom: Spoke VNet address ranges are not advertised to on-premises via ExpressRoute BGP routing table when using hub-spoke topology with VNet Peering, even though the hub/infra VNet address range is correctly advertised
rootCause: VNet peering does not provide transitive routing properties. Spoke VNet addresses peered to a hub VNet are not automatically advertised via BGP to on-premises through ExpressRoute, even if the hub/infra VNet with the ExpressRoute gateway is correctly peered.
solution: Option 1: Peer spokes directly to the infra VNet with ExpressRoute gateway. Option 2: Enable forwarded packets on all VNets, create static routes on the VPN for spoke subnets using the Infra GW BGP address as next hop, and add corresponding firewall/ACL rules. Ensure VNet Peering has Use remote gateway and Allow gateway transit flags enabled.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute BGP Route Advertising and VNet Peering (Transitive Limitations)

symptom: Bidirectional Forwarding Detection (BFD) is not enabled on ExpressRoute circuit despite being configured on customer edge device - link failure detection takes up to 3 minutes instead of sub-second
rootCause: BFD is only enabled by default on circuits created after August 1, 2018 (Private Peering) or January 10, 2020 (Microsoft Peering). Older circuits do not have BFD enabled automatically.
solution: Reset the ExpressRoute circuit peerings to enable BFD. Follow: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/expressroute/expressroute-howto-reset-peering. After reset, configure BFD on both primary and secondary CE/PE devices (interface + BGP session).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Bidirectional Forwarding Detection (BFD)

symptom: Customer asks about BGP graceful restart support with ExpressRoute BFD - wants to maintain forwarding during BGP session restart
rootCause: BGP graceful restart is not supported on ExpressRoute and is not on the product roadmap.
solution: Inform customer that BGP graceful restart is not supported with ExpressRoute. This is a known limitation with no current plans for implementation. Use BFD for fast failure detection and ECMP for redundancy.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Bidirectional Forwarding Detection (BFD)
Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Direct
symptom: ExpressRoute Direct ports not passing traffic after cross-connections established
rootCause: Admin state not enabled on ExpressRoute Direct port links. Must be enabled on each port to pass traffic.
solution: Enable admin state on each port via Portal/PS/CLI. Verify in ASC Resource Explorer.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Direct
symptom: ExpressRoute Direct physical connectivity not established; Tx/Rx light levels outside threshold
rootCause: Light levels outside acceptable range -10dBm to 0dBm, indicating physical connectivity issue.
solution: Check Tx/Rx via Jarvis Dashboard in ASC. If outside range, route to ExpressRoute Operations queue.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Direct
symptom: Customer wants to move ExpressRoute Direct resource across resource groups or subscriptions
rootCause: ExpressRoute Direct does not support move operations across RG/subscriptions.
solution: Must delete and recreate in target RG/subscription. Warning: releases port pair, new resource may get different ports.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Direct
symptom: Customer requests to toggle SCI on ExpressRoute Direct ports
rootCause: Previously required support request. Since 01/2022 customers can configure SCI via PowerShell.
solution: Use PS: Get-AzExpressRoutePort, set Links[0/1].MacSecConfig.SciState="Enabled", Set-AzExpressRoutePort.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Fastpath
symptom: FastPath enabled but no speed/latency improvement; traffic still via gateway
rootCause: MSEE not programmed with /32 static routes for VMs. Traffic continues via standard gateway path.
solution: Check MSEEv2 Route Information dashboard. If no routes, check Route Programming Failure and DirectTunnelWorkItem heartbeat.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Fastpath
symptom: Traffic drop to VM after live migration/redeploy with FastPath enabled
rootCause: After VM migration MSEE has stale /32 route to old host. Old host drops traffic until GatewayManager updates.
solution: Check MSEEv2 Route Validation for mismatched routes. Verify heartbeat (12/min). For Juniper check GRPC failures.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Fastpath
symptom: Cannot enable FastPath; feature unavailable for current gateway SKU
rootCause: FastPath requires Ultra Performance or ErGw3AZ gateway SKU.
solution: Upgrade gateway to Ultra Performance or ErGw3AZ.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Fastpath
symptom: On-prem traffic via FastPath not reaching VIPs on Basic internal load balancer
rootCause: FastPath does not support Basic internal LB. Traffic routes through gateway instead.
solution: Upgrade Basic LB to Standard LB.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Gateway Customer Controlled Maintenance
symptom: Customer-defined maintenance window tied but maintenance did not occur in window
rootCause: Maintenance config and gateway in different regions. Backend cannot find gateway in different region.
solution: Ensure config in same region as gateway. Recreate if mismatched.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Gateway Customer Controlled Maintenance
symptom: Gateway maintenance outside customer-defined window; host update type
rootCause: Host updates on shared hosts not covered by customer-defined windows. By design.
solution: Verify host update via Kusto. Close with By Design.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Gateway Customer Controlled Maintenance
symptom: Cannot set non-daily recurrence for gateway maintenance window
rootCause: VMSS limitation: only daily recurrence supported for Network Gateway.
solution: Only "Day"/"1Day"/"1Days" supported. Close with By Design.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Gateway Customer Controlled Maintenance
symptom: Customer-controlled maintenance windows not working in National/Government clouds
rootCause: MRP team has not pushed notifications for National/Government clouds.
solution: Not supported outside Public cloud. Contact exract team for timeline.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute IPv6 Configuration and Monitoring
symptom: Cannot configure IPv6 private peering; not working for gateway SKU or region
rootCause: IPv6 private peering requires AZ gateway SKU and AZ region. Does not support Global Reach, vWAN, FastPath non-Direct.
solution: Verify AZ SKU, AZ region. Check unsupported combos. Add IPv6 range to GatewaySubnet first.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Monitor
symptom: ExpressRoute metrics missing or gaps in Azure Monitor for specific periods
rootCause: Known issue: SNMP Poller sporadically times out. During timeout metrics not pushed to MDM.
solution: Query ExpressRouteMonitoringLogsTable for MSEE device. Related: ICM-180429571, Bug 6580254.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Resiliency/ExpressRoute Resiliency Guard
symptom: ExpressRoute Virtual Network Gateway with multi-homed resiliency model shows error: Gateway is required to have connections from two ExpressRoute circuits in different peering locations. Connectivity will be disabled.
rootCause: Gateway is configured with multi-homed resiliency model but does not have at least 2 ExpressRoute circuit connections in distinct peering locations, so connectivity is blocked by design.
solution: Either (1) connect a second ExpressRoute circuit in a different peering location to enable BGP/connectivity, or (2) change the gateway resiliency model from Multi-homed to Single-homed if multi-site resilience is not required. Metro circuits bypass this requirement.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Resiliency/ExpressRoute Resiliency Guard
symptom: Deployment fails when attempting to update ExpressRoute gateway from Single-homed to Multi-homed resiliency model. Error: You must connect two or more ExpressRoute circuits in different peering locations before updating to multi-homed.
rootCause: The gateway currently has fewer than 2 circuits connected in different peering locations, which is a prerequisite for multi-homed resiliency model.
solution: Connect at least two ExpressRoute circuits in different peering locations first, then update the resiliency model to Multi-homed.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Resiliency/ExpressRoute Resiliency Guard
symptom: Cannot delete ExpressRoute connection or resource on multi-homed gateway. Error: In order to delete your connection, you must change your ExpressRoute Gateway Resiliency Model to Single-Homed.
rootCause: Multi-homed resiliency model enforces minimum 2 connections in different peering locations; deleting a connection would violate this constraint.
solution: Change the gateway resiliency model to Single-Homed first, then delete the connection or resource.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Resiliency/Resiliency Insights
symptom: ExpressRoute Resiliency Insights shows Score not available at this time after clicking Refresh button repeatedly.
rootCause: Conflict error occurs when handling successive API calls while the first operation is still in progress. The backend cannot handle concurrent computation requests.
solution: Wait for the first operation to complete before clicking Refresh again. Scores and topology are cached for 1 hour; clicking Refresh within 1 hour returns cached data.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Resiliency/Resiliency Insights
symptom: ExpressRoute Resiliency Insights shows Score not available error intermittently. Logs show StorageNotServiceable exception while fetching data from cache blob.
rootCause: Storage calls fail intermittently (StorageNotServiceable), causing the resiliency score computation to fail.
solution: Retry after some time. The storage issue is transient and should resolve on subsequent attempts.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Resiliency/Resiliency Insights
symptom: ExpressRoute Resiliency Insights fails to compute score. Logs show Failed to Fetch Information to compute Route Sets. Adjacency or learned routes are null.
rootCause: The gateway instance used to fetch routing information is randomly picked; if the selected instance is down or under maintenance, the operation fails.
solution: Retry after some time. A different gateway instance will be selected on the next attempt.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Resiliency/Resiliency Insights
symptom: Error when opening ExpressRoute Resiliency Insights blade for the first time in Azure portal.
rootCause: User does not have Contributor or higher RBAC access on the subscription. Resiliency Insights performs a PUT call which requires write permissions.
solution: Assign Contributor or higher RBAC role to the user on the subscription. Refer to https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/vpn-gateway/roles-permissions for RBAC details.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Resiliency/Resiliency Insights
symptom: Number of circuits shown in ExpressRoute Resiliency Insights topology graph does not match actual deployed circuits. Logs show Remote circuit information not found for the given service key.
rootCause: When circuits are deployed in a different region than the gateway, cross-regional calls are made to fetch circuit info. These calls can time out intermittently.
solution: Ask customer to retry after 1 hour. Cross-regional call optimizations are included in GWM release 24.10.

symptom: ExpressRoute Resiliency Validation test shows Invalid status even though the test was never started on that peering location.
rootCause: Known bug: when GET API is first called it stores test details as NotStarted. If a new circuit is added to that peering location afterwards the test is marked as Invalid.
solution: Known product bug tracked by repair item msazure work item 29684376. Inform the customer this is a cosmetic issue and they can safely start a new test.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Resiliency/Resiliency Validation

symptom: ExpressRoute Resiliency Validation home page takes too long to load for customers with many cross-region circuits.
rootCause: GET call uses dump circuit and calls it serially for all cross-region circuits causing slow API response for customers with many cross-region circuits.
solution: Optimization changes completed in PR 10729034 rolling out with build 24.10. Customer may need to wait for rollout. Retrying during off-peak hours may help.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Resiliency/Resiliency Validation

symptom: ExpressRoute failover simulation start operation partially failed with some connections down and others still up.
rootCause: Start operation brings down connections one-by-one sequentially. If error occurs mid-way the operation halts with some connections already down and remaining still up.
solution: 1) Ask customer to click Stop test on portal. 2) If stop fails use STOP Jarvis action via ACIS. 3) If nothing works manually bring BGP up on the device. Check impacted connections blade on portal.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Resiliency/Resiliency Validation

symptom: ExpressRoute failover simulation stop operation fails with error ExpressRouteDeviceUnderMaintenanceDuringStopOperation and customer connections remain down.
rootCause: Device went under maintenance between start and stop operations. Stop cannot bring connections back up while device is offline for maintenance.
solution: 1) Contact ErOps immediately to bring BGP up on at least one device. 2) Keep alert active until device is online. 3) Run Jarvis STOP action to properly stop simulation. Critical scenario as connections down impacts customer traffic.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Resiliency/Resiliency Validation

symptom: Error FailoverSimulationAlreadyInProgress when trying to start ExpressRoute failover simulation on a gateway.
rootCause: A simulation is already running on the same gateway for a different peering location. Only one simulation can run per gateway at a time.
solution: Stop the existing simulation on the other peering location first. Use GET operation or Jarvis Get gateway action to check IsGatewayUnderFailoverSimulation field to find which peering location has active simulation.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Resiliency/Resiliency Validation
Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Scalable Gateway
symptom: Deploying ErGwScale virtual network gateway fails with error stating deployment is not supported in the target region
rootCause: ErGwScale gateway SKU is only available in specific regions. Deploying in unsupported regions triggers a region validation error.
solution: Deploy ErGwScale in a supported region. Check the latest supported region list in the public documentation for ExpressRoute Scalable Gateway.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Scalable Gateway
symptom: Customer wants to migrate from Standard/ErGw1Az, HighPerf/ErGw2Az, or UltraPerf/ErGw3Az gateway SKU to ErGwScale
rootCause: Migration from legacy gateway SKUs to ErGwScale is not supported. ErGwScale requires Standard IP SKU.
solution: Inform customer that migration from legacy SKUs to ErGwScale is not currently supported. Customer needs to deploy a new ErGwScale gateway.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Subscription Operations
symptom: Customer wants to move ExpressRoute circuit or ExpressRoute gateway to another subscription
rootCause: Moving ExpressRoute or ExpressRoute gateway to another subscription is NOT supported by Azure Resource Manager.
solution: Inform customer this operation is not supported. For tenant/directory transfers, plan during maintenance window.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Traffic Collector
symptom: CollectorPolicy update fails with error CollectorPolicyUpdateNotAllowedWhenParentUpdating or CollectorPolicyUpdateNotAllowedWhenUpdating
rootCause: The parent Azure Traffic Collector or the CollectorPolicy itself is currently being updated by a previous operation.
solution: Wait for the ongoing operation to complete before retrying the CollectorPolicy update.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Traffic Collector
symptom: Creating CollectorPolicy fails with AzureTrafficCollectorAlreadyHasCollectorPolicyConfigured
rootCause: Each Azure Traffic Collector can only have one CollectorPolicy configured at a time.
solution: Remove or update the existing CollectorPolicy before creating a new one.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Traffic Collector
symptom: Configuring CollectorPolicy fails with AzureTrafficCollectorAndIngestionSourceSubscriptionMismatch
rootCause: Azure Traffic Collector and ingestion source are in different subscriptions.
solution: Ensure Azure Traffic Collector and ExpressRoute Direct circuit are in the same subscription.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Traffic Collector
symptom: Configuring CollectorPolicy fails with AzureTrafficCollectorOnlySupportedForCircuitsOnExpressRouteDirect
rootCause: Azure Traffic Collector only supports ExpressRoute Direct circuits.
solution: Azure Traffic Collector can only be used with ExpressRoute Direct circuits. Check latest docs for provider port support.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/ExpressRoute Traffic Collector
symptom: Configuring CollectorPolicy fails with AzureTrafficCollectorNotSupportedForCircuitsInRegion
rootCause: Azure Traffic Collector is not available in the region of the ExpressRoute Direct circuit.
solution: Deploy in a supported region or wait for regional expansion.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/Traffic Block Feature
symptom: Cannot connect between two virtual networks over ExpressRoute after deploying new gateways (Y-pattern). Prefixes tagged with communities 65515:10000 or 65515:20000.
rootCause: New ExpressRoute gateways have traffic block feature enabled by default restricting Y patterns. Both gateways in vNets causes vNet-to-vNet traffic drop.
solution: Enable allow traffic from remote vNets on BOTH gateways via Portal (VNet Gateway > Configuration) or PowerShell.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/Traffic Block Feature
symptom: Cannot connect between vNet and vWAN over ExpressRoute after deploying new gateways. Traffic Drop shows vNet-to-vWAN block.
rootCause: New gateways have traffic block enabled by default. One gateway in vNet and one in vWAN causes traffic drop.
solution: On vNet gateway: enable allow traffic from other vWANs. On vWAN hub: mark allow traffic from non-vWANs. Both sides required.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Features and Functions/Route Filter for Microsoft Peering
symptom: Attaching route filter with Microsoft 365 services to ExpressRoute circuit fails
rootCause: Customer must have authorization to consume M365 services through ExpressRoute.
solution: Customer needs O365 over ExpressRoute authorization. Validate via ADO wiki: How to validate customers for ExR O365 Communities.

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/Delete ExR circuit with stale connection entries
symptom: ExpressRoute circuit deletion fails with ConflictError: "The specified bgp peering is in use. Please delete the links associated with the specified service key and try again" but no connections visible in circuit dump or NRP subscription details (connections array empty)
rootCause: Stale connection entries exist in backend VnetConfig Kusto table but are not visible through normal circuit dump or NRP subscription details
solution: Query VnetConfig table in hybridnetworking Kusto cluster (aznwmds database) using the circuit ServiceKey to find stale VNET/gateway associations. Delete the stale connection from the Virtual Network gateway blade. Retry circuit deletion. If deletion still fails, escalate to TA to open ICM with EEE Cloudnet team

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/ExpressRoute BGP Down Root Cause Analysis
symptom: ExpressRoute BGP session goes down; syslog shows BFD session down with DETECT TIMER EXPIRED (Cisco: BFDFSM-6-BFD_SESS_DOWN; Juniper: BFD Session state Up -> Down, CtlExpire)
rootCause: BFD (Bidirectional Forwarding Detection) detect timer expired indicating link failure in ExpressRoute connection path. Layer-2 device failure between CE and MSEE not detected by BGP alone
solution: Query SyslogData in azphynet Kusto cluster for BFD events on MSEE devices. Investigate physical link or intermediate L2 device issues. Check BFD timers configuration. Verify alternate connection path availability

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/ExpressRoute BGP Down Root Cause Analysis
symptom: ExpressRoute BGP session down with hold time expired notification in syslog (Cisco: BGP-3-NOTIFICATION received hold time expired; Juniper: Hold Timer Expired Error, holdtime expired)
rootCause: BGP keepalive messages not received within configured hold time period, indicating connectivity or routing issues between BGP peers
solution: Query SyslogData for hold timer events. Verify BGP keepalive (default 60s) and hold-time (default 180s) configuration. Investigate network connectivity between CE and MSEE. Check for packet loss or high latency on the path

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/ExpressRoute BGP Down Root Cause Analysis
symptom: ExpressRoute BGP session reset with Peer over prefix limit or MAXPFXEXCEED in syslog; neighbor removed from session; prefix count exceeds configured maximum (e.g. 1001 exceeds limit 1000)
rootCause: Number of received BGP prefixes exceeded configured maximum prefix limit. Common cause: customer re-advertises Microsoft Peering prefixes back over Private peering, causing prefix count to spike after Microsoft peering update
solution: Query SyslogData for MAXPFXEXCEED or prefix_thresh_exceeded events. Review customer routing configuration to stop re-advertising Microsoft Peering prefixes over Private peering. Increase prefix limit if the additional prefixes are legitimate

sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/ExpressRoute BGP Down Root Cause Analysis
symptom: ExpressRoute interface goes down with syslog showing link down (Cisco: SPA-6-UPDOWN link down due to remote fault; Juniper: ifOperStatus down(2), SNMP_TRAP_LINK_DOWN)
rootCause: Physical interface flap on MSEE device, can be caused by remote fault, physical layer issues, or hardware problems on provider/customer side
solution: Query SyslogData for interface events (SPA-6-UPDOWN for Cisco, ifOperStatus for Juniper). Investigate physical connectivity and fiber/cable integrity. Check for hardware issues on provider or customer CE equipment. Coordinate with provider if remote fault indicated

symptom: ExpressRoute Direct desired bandwidth is unavailable at the target peering location; customer cannot provision the requested port size (10G/100G) at their chosen location
rootCause: No available ExpressRoute Direct bandwidth/capacity at the target peering location; all ports of the requested size are allocated
solution: Validate unavailability using PowerShell (Get-AzExpressRoutePortsLocation -LocationName) or CLI (az network express-route port location show). Gather customer requirements (alternate location feasibility, timeline, future capacity needs). For 10G, suggest service provider model as alternative. Post to ExpressRoute Teams channel for TA approval, then create IcM using ASC template 'ExpressRoute Direct Capacity Unavailable'. For customers with CSAM, initiate UAT process.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/ExpressRoute Direct Capacity is Unavailable at the Target Peering Location

symptom: MACsec on ExpressRoute Direct: no received MKA packets on MSEE, only transmitted packets; MACsec session not establishing; BGP and data path down after enabling MACsec
rootCause: Layer-2 device (switch) exists between customer ExpressRoute edge device and MSEE; MACsec requires point-to-point Layer 2 connection with no intermediary devices
solution: Have customer validate their physical path topology. MACsec is layer-2 point-to-point encryption - no layer-2 devices (switches) between customer external interface and MSEE. Check MKA statistics via Jarvis Actions: Brooklyn > ExR Diagnostic Operations > Run Any Read Only Command > show security mka statistics interface <interface>. Zero received with nonzero transmitted = physical topology issue.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/ExpressRoute Direct Ports MACsec

symptom: MACsec on ExpressRoute Direct: CAK mismatch packets counter increasing in MKA statistics; MACsec session fails to establish or drops
rootCause: Customer configured different CKN or CAK values on their device than stored in Azure Key Vault; connectivity association key mismatch between MSEE and customer edge
solution: Check MKA statistics via Jarvis Actions (show security mka statistics interface <interface>). If CAK mismatch packets increasing, ask customer to verify CKN and CAK match Key Vault values. If ICV mismatch packets increasing, CAK does not match on both ends. Have customer re-enter values if necessary.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/ExpressRoute Direct Ports MACsec

symptom: Customer wants to enable MACsec on ExpressRoute Direct but ports are on Cisco MSEE devices; MACsec configuration fails or is unavailable
rootCause: MACsec on Cisco MSEE is not supported. Legacy ExpressRoute Direct used both Cisco and Juniper, but MACsec only works on Juniper MSEEs
solution: Customer must recreate ExpressRoute Direct resource so it lands on Juniper MSEE devices. MACsec only supported on Juniper MSEEs. Identify MSEE type from ASC (Cisco: ash-06gmr-cis-*, Juniper: exr01.*).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/ExpressRoute Direct Ports MACsec

symptom: MACsec enabled on ExpressRoute Direct but traffic fails between Cisco on-prem device and Juniper MSEE; ARP learned on Juniper but Cisco fails to learn ARP; MACsec shows UP
rootCause: Cisco has include-sci (Secure Channel Identifier) enabled by default, while Juniper does not. SCI tagging mismatch causes ARP resolution failure between endpoints.
solution: Enable SCI on ExpressRoute Direct ports: $erDirect.Links[0].MacSecConfig.SciState = 'Enabled'; $erDirect.Links[1].MacSecConfig.SciState = 'Enabled'; Set-AzExpressRoutePort. Requires API version 2021-05-01+. Adds include-sci to Juniper MSEE MACsec config.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/ExpressRoute Direct Ports MACsec

symptom: Set-AzExpressRoutePort fails with Error mapping types when enabling MACsec on ExpressRoute Direct; error references PSExpressRoutePort to ExpressRoutePort mapping, Property: Links
rootCause: Bug in newer versions of Az.Network PowerShell module causing type mapping failure for MACsec configuration on ExpressRoute port links
solution: Downgrade Az.Network PowerShell module to version 2.4.0: Install-Module -Name Az.Network -RequiredVersion 2.4.0 -Force, then retry MACsec configuration.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/ExpressRoute Direct Ports MACsec

symptom: MACsec Key Vault secret creation fails or MACsec configuration fails when secret value name contains underscores
rootCause: Azure Key Vault secret names for MACsec CKN/CAK do not support underscore characters
solution: Remove underscores from Key Vault secret name. Use only alphanumeric characters and hyphens in Set-AzKeyVaultSecret -Name parameter.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/ExpressRoute Direct Ports MACsec

symptom: MACsec configuration on ExpressRoute Direct fails with NetconfException: ckn Must be an even-length string up to 64 hexadecimal digits; CAK/CKN value rejected by MSEE
rootCause: CKN or CAK value does not meet format requirements: CKN must be even-length up to 64 hex digits; CAK must be even-length up to 32 hex digits (GcmAes128) or 64 hex digits (GcmAes256)
solution: Ensure CKN is even-length string of up to 64 hex digits (0-9, A-F). For CAK: up to 32 hex digits for GcmAes128/GcmAesXpn128 or 64 for GcmAes256/GcmAesXpn256. Recommend full-length keys (32 or 64 chars) as shorter keys are zero-padded internally.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/ExpressRoute Direct Ports MACsec

symptom: ExpressRoute BGP peering down; customer configured same ASN for both local and remote BGP peers instead of using Microsoft ASN 12076
rootCause: Customer misconfigured BGP by using the same AS number for both local and remote peers. The remote peer ASN for ExpressRoute must be 12076.
solution: Have customer correct BGP config to use ASN 12076 as remote peer ASN. Verify via syslog: cluster('azphynet.kusto.windows.net').database('azdhmds').SyslogData filtered by device name and peer IP.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/ExpressRoute Down

symptom: ExpressRoute BGP not establishing; syslog shows BADAUTH No MD5 digest or Invalid MD5 digest; shared key/MD5 hash mismatch between MSEE and customer edge
rootCause: Shared key/MD5 hash configured on only one side, or passwords do not match. Max 25 alphanumeric characters, special characters not supported.
solution: Check DumpCircuitInfo for SHARED KEY EXISTS. Check syslog for BADAUTH errors. No MD5 digest = not configured on customer side. Invalid MD5 digest = passwords mismatch. Advise: remove shared key from both sides, or ensure identical key on both Azure peering and on-prem.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/ExpressRoute Down

symptom: ExpressRoute BGP between MSEE and Gateway not establishing; gateway not learning routes from on-premises; NSG or UDR on gateway subnet
rootCause: NSG on gateway subnet blocking BGP, or UDR with default route (0.0.0.0/0) on gateway subnet redirecting traffic away from MSEE
solution: Remove NSG from gateway subnet. Remove UDR from gateway subnet (especially default route). Even non-default UDR routes can cause issues. Run DebugBot from ASC Diagnostics tab to auto-detect these problems.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/ExpressRoute Down

symptom: ExpressRoute gateway SKU scale-out fails or customer asks about downtime for gateway SKU change; insufficient subnet space or CPU cores
rootCause: Gateway subnet may not have enough IP address space (each instance needs IP; 13 reserved). Gateway subscription may not have enough CPU cores for VM SKU.
solution: Pre-checks: 1) Verify gateway subnet from ASC, available IPs = total - 13. 2) Find VM SKU from aka.ms/ergwdashboard, check CPU cores from Azure docs. 3) Verify cores in gateway subscription via Kusto: cluster('hybridnetworking.kusto.windows.net').database('aznwmds').GatewaySubscriptions. Ensure sufficient IPs and cores.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/ExpressRoute Gateway Scale Out Prechecks

symptom: Azure Monitor shows ExpressRoute BGP availability as down during maintenance, but customer confirms BGP UP on their end; conflicting status
rootCause: During non-disruptive MSEE maintenance, device is marked offline suppressing SNMP polling. This causes Azure metrics to falsely show BGP down while session remains active.
solution: Verify BGP logs to confirm session UP during maintenance. Communicate: BGP was UP, SNMP polling was paused as device marked offline, causing metric to appear down. This is known behaviour. Validate maintenance via Kusto: cluster('azwan').database('OneWan').ExpressRouteCommsLog.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/ExpressRoute Maintenance

symptom: Traffic disruption during ExpressRoute maintenance despite redundant circuits; traffic does not fail over to redundant path during planned MSEE maintenance
rootCause: Customer not honoring AS-path prepend applied by Microsoft (12076 prepended 8 times). Other BGP attributes (local preference, weight, MED) may override AS-path length decision.
solution: Verify customer honors AS-path prepend. Check if other BGP attributes override AS-path. Confirm maintenance timeline via Kusto: cluster('phynetval').database('aznwmds').AzureAaaMasterSessions filtered by as-path prepend. Response for custom procedures: We cannot implement customer-specific procedures as these are shared devices.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/ExpressRoute Maintenance

symptom: Customer reports packet loss or performance degradation on ExpressRoute circuit after rate limiting feature rollout (started July 2023). QoS dropped packets metrics show non-zero values.
rootCause: Customer circuit traffic exceeds configured bandwidth, triggering rate limiting hard policing. Rate limiting does not shape traffic but drops packets when throughput bursts over the configured bandwidth limit at sub-second intervals.
solution: 1) Check QoS drop metrics (QosDropBitsInPerSecond/QosDropBitsOutPerSecond) in portal or Kusto ERMetricsLogsTable. 2) Compare BitsInPerSecond/BitsOutPerSecond with circuit bandwidth. 3) If customer is exceeding bandwidth, advise to increase circuit bandwidth via portal (takes effect in seconds, cannot be decreased later). 4) Note: metrics are averaged over 1-5 min intervals so traffic may appear within limits while sub-second bursts cause drops.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/ExpressRoute Rate Limiting

symptom: Customer wants to decrease ExpressRoute circuit bandwidth after a previous increase, but the option is not available in the Azure portal.
rootCause: ExpressRoute does not support decreasing bandwidth on an existing circuit. This is a platform limitation by design.
solution: Create a new ExpressRoute circuit with the desired lower bandwidth, migrate traffic to the new circuit, then delete the old circuit.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/ExpressRoute Rate Limiting

symptom: Rate limiting QoS drops observed on ExpressRoute circuit even though customer averaged traffic metrics (BitsInPerSecond/BitsOutPerSecond) show usage within the configured bandwidth limit.
rootCause: Metrics data is averaged over 1-minute/5-minute intervals, masking sub-second traffic bursts that exceed the configured bandwidth. Rate limiting uses hard policing at sub-second intervals, so bursts can trigger drops even if averaged metrics look normal.
solution: 1) Explain to customer that averaged metrics can mask sub-second bursts. 2) Compare DroppedBitsInPerSecond/DroppedBitsOutPerSecond with traffic metrics. 3) If drops occur without any bursting, this may indicate a data-path issue requiring further debugging with TA assistance.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/ExpressRoute Rate Limiting

symptom: ExpressRoute circuit goes down. ISP claims Azure swapped the IPs between primary and secondary links on the MSEE subinterfaces, causing loss of BGP connectivity.
rootCause: Azure did not swap the IPs. No ARM/NRP operations indicate peering was updated. The customer/ISP made changes on their side.
solution: Use Kusto query on SubIntfTable (cluster: Hybridnetworking, database: aznwmds) to show historical IP assignments over 90 days: SubIntfTable | where ServiceKey == '<key>' | where PreciseTimeStamp > ago(90d) | project PreciseTimeStamp, DeviceName, IpAddress, NetworkMask, InterfaceName, State, CTag, STag. This proves Azure-side IP stability and shifts responsibility to ISP/customer.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/Historical MSEE Subinterface Information

symptom: Customer ExpressRoute Microsoft peering is not coming up. AdvertisedPublicPrefixesState shows ValidationNeeded in Get-AzExpressRouteCircuitPeeringConfig output or in Dump Circuit Info.
rootCause: Customer ASN and public prefixes are not properly registered in the Internet Routing Registry (ARIN/APNIC/AFRINIC/LACNIC/RIPENCC/RADB/ALTDB). Automatic validation by Microsoft failed because the assignment is not recorded at the routing registry.
solution: 1) Verify ASN and each public prefix at the registrar (e.g. whois.arin.net). 2) Check Net Type is Direct Assignment. 3) If owned by another entity, request Letter of Authorization (LoA). 4) Validate customer contact email matches organization. 5) Post to ExpressRoute Teams channel with required info (Resource ID, Service Key, Dump Circuit, Dump Routing, LOA) for TA manual validation.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/How to Manually Validate ASN and Public Prefixes for ExR

symptom: Azure portal displays only 1 diagnostic setting for ExpressRoute Traffic Collector, even when multiple diagnostic settings have been configured via CLI (PowerShell New-AzDiagnosticSetting).
rootCause: By design, the Azure portal UI only renders a single diagnostic setting for ExpressRoute Traffic Collector resources. CLI-created additional settings exist but are not surfaced in the portal.
solution: Use CLI (PowerShell Get-AzDiagnosticSetting) to view all configured diagnostic settings. To manage them, use New-AzDiagnosticSetting and Remove-AzDiagnosticSetting commands.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/How to check expressroute traffic collector diagnostic settings

symptom: ExpressRoute Traffic Collector diagnostic settings still reference a deleted Log Analytics workspace. The portal shows the workspace as the log destination, but the workspace no longer exists.
rootCause: Azure does not automatically remove or update diagnostic settings when the target Log Analytics workspace is deleted. The stale reference persists in the Traffic Collector configuration.
solution: Remove the stale diagnostic setting using PowerShell: Remove-AzDiagnosticSetting. Then optionally reconfigure with a valid workspace using New-AzDiagnosticSetting.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/How to check expressroute traffic collector diagnostic settings

symptom: Traffic from Azure PaaS resources routes via the public Internet instead of returning through ExpressRoute Microsoft Peering, causing asymmetric routing or traffic drops.
rootCause: The Azure resource Public IP has routing preference set to 'Internet' (ISP network) instead of 'Microsoft Global Network'. Resources with Internet routing preference send return traffic via ISP, not ExpressRoute. Public IP routing preference cannot be changed after creation.
solution: Create a new Public IP with routing preference set to 'Microsoft Global Network' (default) and reassign it to the resource. This ensures return traffic routes through the Microsoft backbone and back via ExpressRoute. Note: only VM, VMSS, AKS, LB, AppGW, and Azure Firewall support Internet routing preference.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/Microsoft Peering Connection Issues

symptom: ExpressRoute Microsoft Peering shows ADVERTISED PREFIXES STATE as 'ValidationNeeded' in Dump Circuit output. Customer prefixes are not being advertised to MSEE.
rootCause: The customer's advertised public prefixes have not been validated against the Routing Registry (ARIN/RIPE/etc). The prefix-to-ASN ownership has not been verified.
solution: Follow the manual ASN and public prefix validation process: generate private/public key pair, upload certificate to Internet Routing Registry (IRR), sign the validation ID with the private key, and submit the base64 signature via portal or API.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/Microsoft Peering Connection Issues

symptom: ExpressRoute Microsoft Peering BGP sessions are unstable, with frequent session drops and re-establishments.
rootCause: MTU size mismatch between the on-premises router and the MSEE. Packets exceeding the smaller MTU get fragmented or dropped, causing BGP keepalive failures.
solution: Set the MTU to 1500 on the on-premises router interface facing the MSEE. Ensure both sides use matching MTU values to prevent fragmentation-related BGP instability.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/Microsoft Peering Connection Issues

symptom: On-premises network is not learning O365 routes via ExpressRoute Microsoft Peering. Dump Circuit shows 'O365 SERVICES AUTHORIZED: False'.
rootCause: The ExpressRoute circuit subscription has not been authorized for Office 365 services. Microsoft blocks O365 route filters by default and requires an approval process through the customer's Microsoft account team.
solution: Customer must work with their Microsoft account team to go through the O365 ExpressRoute authorization process. The account team submits the request, and once approved, route filters for O365 communities can be added. Direct customer requests without account team involvement are not accepted.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/Microsoft Peering Connection Issues

symptom: Azure Support Center (ASC) shows error: 'NRP Service Provider Circuit Provisioning and Brooklyn Service Provider Circuit Provisioning differ' for an ExpressRoute circuit.
rootCause: The Gateway Manager (Brooklyn) data store and NRP data store have become out-of-sync. Gateway Manager is the authoritative source for ExpressRoute circuit configuration, but occasionally the two stores diverge.
solution: Instruct customer to perform a write operation to re-sync: click Refresh on the ExpressRoute circuit in portal, or run PowerShell: $ckt = Get-AzExpressRouteCircuit -Name '<name>' -ResourceGroupName '<rg>'; Set-AzExpressRouteCircuit -ExpressRouteCircuit $ckt. Doc: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/expressroute/reset-circuit
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/NRP Circuit Provisioning and Brooklyn Circuit Provisioning Differ

symptom: ExpressRoute Public Peering experiencing SNAT exhaustion. NAT translation count exceeds 64,000 (checked via ACIS 'ip nat translations vrf <VRF> total'). Connections to Azure services fail or become unreliable.
rootCause: Public Peering has only 1 NAT IP address per BGP session (primary and secondary each get 1 NAT IP). With a single IP, the maximum NAT translations is approximately 64k. When refcount exceeds 64k, SNAT port exhaustion occurs.
solution: Migrate from Public Peering to Microsoft Peering, which allows multiple NAT IP addresses per BGP session. Public peering has been deprecated and is not available for new circuits. For existing circuits, Microsoft Peering provides better scalability for NAT.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/Public Peering Check Total NAT Translations

symptom: ExpressRoute authorization key appears completely masked under connections; customer cannot identify which auth key is used by which connection for audit purposes
rootCause: Certificate rotation causes the certificate used to encrypt and store the authorization key to be rotated. Once rotated, the auth key stored in NRP KVS cannot be decrypted, resulting in CryptographicException: The enveloped-data message does not contain the specified recipient
solution: Use Kusto query against hybridnetworking cluster aznwmds database to find the unmasked authorization key linked to the connection by querying with resource location and resource ID
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/Retrieve ExpressRoute connection Authorization Key

symptom: ExpressRoute circuit data in Azure Portal/PowerShell/CLI differs from Dump Circuit data; ASC findings show NRP Circuit Provisioning and Brooklyn Circuit Provisioning differ
rootCause: Gateway Manager data store (authoritative source) and NRP data store got out-of-sync. Portal/CLI shows NRP data while Dump Circuit shows Gateway Manager data
solution: Customer performs a write operation to force sync: use Portal refresh button on ExpressRoute circuit overview page, or run Get-AzExpressRouteCircuit then Set-AzExpressRouteCircuit in PowerShell
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/Sync NRP with Gateway Manager

symptom: iPerf3 shows low throughput (~47 Mbits/sec on 1 Gbps link or proportionally low on larger circuits) when testing ExpressRoute performance with default settings on Windows VMs
rootCause: iPerf3 uses its own hardcoded TCP window settings with a low TCP Window Scale Factor shift count of 2, instead of using the OS TCP autotuning settings (shift count 8). This limits the amount of data in flight and reduces throughput, especially on high-latency or high-bandwidth links
solution: Use iPerf3 with explicit TCP window size: iperf3 -c <IP> -w 16776K. This forces a higher TCP Window Scale Factor (shift count 9), allowing more data in flight and saturating the link. For large circuits (10 Gbps+), also run simultaneous iPerf instances from different source/destination pairs and use large VM SKUs
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/How To/TSG - How to properly measure ER throughput using IPERF

symptom: Traffic from Azure VM to on-premise reaches on-prem firewall and ICMP echo replies are seen at firewall, but replies are lost in transit and never received by source Azure VM over ExpressRoute Private peering
rootCause: Multiple VNets linked to ExpressRoute have overlapping address prefixes with different subnet mask lengths, causing routing conflicts where return traffic is routed to the wrong VNet
solution: Remove the linked VNet with the shortest subnet mask to eliminate the overlapping address prefix. Use Dump Circuit to match VNet with tunnel GRE and MSEE KEY, then verify with Jarvis Debug ACL on both portchannel and tunnel interfaces to confirm traffic flow
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Review/ExR Private peering VNET overlapping addr. prefix

symptom: ExpressRoute circuit enters Failed provisioning state after update (PUT operation)
rootCause: Various causes including stale backend state, misconfigured peering, or failed async operation on the circuit
solution: Run a PUT operation on the circuit to re-trigger provisioning: Get-AzExpressRouteCircuit then Set-AzExpressRouteCircuit, or use Azure Resource Explorer to Edit and PUT the circuit resource
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Review/ExpressRoute Circuit in a Failed State After Update

symptom: ExpressRoute circuit update or delete fails when circuit has outstanding authorizations to other subscriptions
rootCause: The circuit has active authorizations linking it to VNets in other subscriptions, preventing modifications
solution: Remove the authorizations using Remove-AzExpressRouteCircuitAuthorization, then retry the circuit update or delete operation
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Review/ExpressRoute Circuit in a Failed State After Update

symptom: ExpressRoute circuit delete fails because virtual network is still linked to the circuit
rootCause: VNet connections to the ExpressRoute circuit must be removed before the circuit can be deleted
solution: Verify connected VNets via Dump Circuit, delete the VNet-to-ExR connection, then retry the circuit delete
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Review/ExpressRoute Circuit in a Failed State After Update

symptom: ExpressRoute circuit fails when configuring Microsoft Peering with Standard SKU
rootCause: Microsoft Peering requires Premium SKU; Standard SKU does not support it
solution: Upgrade the ExpressRoute circuit SKU from Standard to Premium using Set-AzExpressRouteCircuit with Sku.Tier=Premium
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Review/ExpressRoute Circuit in a Failed State After Update

symptom: ExpressRoute circuit update fails with Insufficient Capacity error at the peering location
rootCause: The region/peering location has exceeded 80% capacity on BrkGtw, insufficient bandwidth to fulfill the circuit update
solution: Confirm via MDM NRP Frontend (Insufficient Capacity) and BrkGtw async queries; open ICM to PG for capacity expansion; optionally downgrade circuit or create a second circuit at a nearby peering location
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Review/ExpressRoute Circuit in a Failed State After Update

symptom: ExpressRoute circuit enters failed state with error: The specified bgp shared key length was not valid
rootCause: The BGP shared key (PSK) length exceeds 25 characters or is empty
solution: Have customer verify BGP PSK length on their local router (or service provider) is under 25 characters; may require ICM for MSEE table cleanup if stale config exists
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Review/ExpressRoute Circuit in a Failed State After Update

symptom: Cannot delete ExpressRoute circuit because service provider provisioning state shows Enabled/Disabling
rootCause: The service provider has not yet deprovisioned the circuit on their side
solution: Customer must work with their service provider to deprovision the circuit first; once provider provisioning state is Not Provisioned, run the delete cmdlet
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Review/ExpressRoute Circuit in a Failed State After Update

symptom: ExpressRoute circuit enters Failed state after bandwidth upgrade attempt with error: Insufficient bandwidth available to perform this circuit update operation
rootCause: Known bug (BUG 1412573): When upgrading bandwidth, the backend may still reference old bandwidth causing InvalidOperation. PG has no current plan to fix this - requires coordination with service provider for both-side config update.
solution: Recreate the ExpressRoute circuit with the desired bandwidth. There is no in-place fix; this is a known limitation per PG.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Review/ExpressRoute Circuit in a Failed State After Update

symptom: After ExpressRoute link failure, traffic to certain on-premises subnets is unreachable despite having IPsec S2S VPN as backup
rootCause: Customer did not include all on-premises subnets in their IPsec S2S tunnel local gateway configuration. When ExR is active, more specific BGP routes mask the missing IPsec routes. When ExR fails, those subnets have no backup path.
solution: Ensure all on-premises subnets needing failover are included in the IPsec S2S tunnel local gateway prefix list. Verify using VFP getvfp command to check RouteTargetVpn rules for both ExR (with GRE key) and IPsec (without GRE key) paths.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Review/ExpressRoute with IPsec tunnel Failover Internals

symptom: Azure Site Recovery (ASR) replication over ExpressRoute (Microsoft/Public Peering) shows sluggish performance; customer reports not getting full bandwidth
rootCause: On-premises VM sends TCP segments with MSS larger than 1440 bytes, causing fragmentation across Azure infrastructure (VIP/Load Balancer). Secondary ExR circuit was not enabled, limiting aggregate throughput to a single link.
solution: Clamp on-premises VM or edge router MSS to 1440 bytes (or set MTU to 1480). Enable secondary ExpressRoute circuit for load distribution. Use iPerf with VIP to test both directions and verify performance improvement.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Review/TSG: ASR (Azure Recovery Services) Sluggish Performance over ExR

symptom: ExpressRoute circuit Resource Health blade shows Unknown status or flip-flops between Available and Unknown
rootCause: Resource Health monitoring backend fails to complete HTTP outgoing request to ARM with RequestTimeout error, unable to fetch circuit health data from AzureERShoebox
solution: Check AzureERShoebox health status in JARVIS; verify ResourceProviderHealthSystemLogs and ResourceProviderRequestLogs tables for failed events; escalate to Teams if Shoebox is unhealthy or failed events persist.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure ExpressRoute/Review/Troubleshooting Resource Health Monitoring for ExpressRoute Circuit

symptom: Virtual Machine in Azure Extended Zone cannot connect to the Internet; outbound connections fail
rootCause: By design, VMs in Extended Zones do not have default outbound access. An APIPA range address (169.254.0.0/16) is assigned as Pseudo VIP, which is blocked by VFP rule BLOCK_HLIP_TO_EXTERNAL_IPV4_RULE.
solution: Use explicit outbound methods for Internet connectivity: NAT Gateway, Load Balancer with outbound rules, or Public IP directly assigned to the VM.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Extended Zones/TSG: Cannot connect to Internet from a Virtual Machine in Extended Zones

symptom: Azure Firewall Manager shows spoke VNet is not connected to any firewall, even though the spoke is peered to a hub VNet that has a firewall deployed. The Firewall Manager UI incorrectly displays "No Firewall Deployed" for the spoke.
rootCause: An empty Azure Firewall subnet (AzureFirewallSubnet) exists in the spoke VNet, or the spoke VNet has no subnets at all. This causes Firewall Manager to fail to detect the hub firewall connection via VNet peering.
solution: Delete the empty AzureFirewallSubnet in the spoke VNet, or add at least one subnet to an empty spoke VNet. Verify that the hub VNet actually has a firewall deployed. After the fix, Firewall Manager will correctly show the spoke is connected to the hub firewall. Note: This is a cosmetic issue only - the actual VNet peering and UDR-based firewall connectivity remains functional.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Firewall/Azure Firewall Manager

symptom: Deploying multiple Azure IP Groups simultaneously using Bicep or ARM template results in only one IP Group being created successfully while others enter a failed state.
rootCause: IP Group resources cannot be deployed in parallel. Concurrent PUT operations on multiple IP Groups in the same resource group cause conflicts, resulting in deployment failures for all but one resource.
solution: Use the @batchSize(1) decorator in Bicep to force serial deployment of IP Groups. Define an array of IP Group configurations and iterate using a for-loop with @batchSize(1), ensuring each IP Group is fully deployed before the next one begins. Example: define param ipGroupsArray with name and ipAddresses, then use @batchSize(1) before the resource declaration with [for item in ipGroupsArray: {...}].
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Firewall/Azure IP Groups/Steps to deploy Ip Group Serially using Bicep

symptom: Upgrading Azure Firewall classic policy to premium policy fails with error: "LinkedAuthorizationFailed - The linked property properties.ruleCollections[*].rules[*].sourceIpGroups[*] has exceeded the maximum allowed scopes. The maximum allowed linked scopes is 2000."
rootCause: ARM enforces a hard limit (LinkedResourceCheck) of 2000 source IP Groups and 2000 destination IP Groups per ruleCollectionGroup. When the total referenced IP Groups in a single ruleCollectionGroup exceed this limit, the deployment is rejected.
solution: Split the firewall rules into multiple Rule Collection Groups so that no single group references more than 2000 source IP Groups or 2000 destination IP Groups. To calculate the current count: download the network rules Excel from ASC, filter the source/destination IP Group columns (uncheck "not configured", "N/A", and blanks), then count the remaining entries.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Firewall/Azure IP Groups/TSG - IP Group Limit

symptom: Destination servers reject Azure Firewall outbound connections due to SLB port reuse. Destination firewalls/applications have WAITTIME before accepting new connections from same source IP and port, causing connection failures.
rootCause: Azure SLB immediately reuses SNAT ports after TCP/UDP conversation closes (by design). Port selection is not sequential but uses FIFO-like method. No configuration to adjust reuse timing.
solution: Deploy NAT Gateway with Azure Firewall to provide a wider SNAT port pool, reducing the chance of rapid port reuse. Ref: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/firewall/integrate-with-nat-gateway
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Firewall/Features & Functions/AZFW and SLB - Port Re-use Behavior

symptom: Security scan (NMAP) reports unexpected open/listening ports on Azure Firewall private IP (e.g., 80, 443, 1433). PCI compliance scan flags these as security vulnerabilities.
rootCause: Azure Firewall creates proxy listeners on ports specified in Application Rules (e.g., mssql:1433 creates listener on port 1433). This is by-design behavior for application-layer inspection.
solution: Review Application Rules to identify which protocols/ports create active listeners. Explain to customer this is by-design Azure Firewall proxy behavior, not a security vulnerability.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Firewall/Features & Functions/Application Rules - Active Listeners on the AZFW

symptom: Client cannot connect to HTTPS destination via Azure Firewall. SSL/TLS handshake fails. Bypassing Azure Firewall resolves the issue. Pcap shows wildcard ServerName (e.g., *.threatpulse.com) in ClientHello.
rootCause: Client sends wildcard SNI instead of actual hostname in TLS ServerName extension, violating RFC 6066. Azure Firewall correctly rejects traffic with invalid SNI.
solution: Fix client application to send actual hostname in TLS SNI extension, not wildcard. Verify by checking pcap for ServerName value in ClientHello. Adding wildcard FQDN to application rules does not resolve this.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Firewall/Features & Functions/Azure Firewall & SNI

symptom: Customer confused about Azure Firewall Data Processed billing metric. Unsure whether it equals inbound+outbound total or just metered bytes.
rootCause: Azure Firewall uses inbound data only as the Data Processed value for billing. Outbound/blocked data is not counted separately.
solution: Only inbound data counts toward Data Processed billing. Example: 2GB in, 0.5GB blocked, 1.5GB out = 2GB processed data (inbound only).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Firewall/Features & Functions/Azure Firewall - "Data Processed" value for billing purpose

symptom: In VWAN Secure Hub with Private Traffic secured, traffic between subnets in same spoke VNet does not route through Azure Firewall and stays local.
rootCause: VNet-local routes are more specific than catch-all RFC 1918 ranges. Adding VNet/subnet ranges to Firewall Manager PRIVATE PREFIXES does not override VNet-local routes.
solution: Manually create UDR/Route Table on VNet subnets pointing VNet address range to Azure Firewall Private ILB IP. Do NOT use Firewall Manager Private Prefixes for intra-VNet routing.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Firewall/Features & Functions/Firewall Manager - Using Secure Hub (VWAN) Routing

symptom: When both Security Partner (internet) and Azure Firewall (private traffic) are configured in VWAN Secure Hub, VM effective routes show 0.0.0.0/0 next-hop as Azure Firewall instead of VPN Gateway/Security Partner.
rootCause: By design to prevent asymmetric routing. All traffic routes through AZFW first, then internet traffic egresses AZFW to VPN GW and Security Partner.
solution: Configure internet traffic allow rules on Azure Firewall in addition to Security Partner rules. Inform customer this is expected by-design routing behavior.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Firewall/Features & Functions/Firewall Manager - Using Secure Hub (VWAN) Routing

symptom: Approximately 90% performance degradation when Azure Firewall IDPS is set to Alert+Deny mode. Significant latency increase for packet processing.
rootCause: In Alert+Deny mode, every packet must go through IDPS engine inline. Same packet may traverse IDPS multiple times (e.g., FTP with TLS: IDPS->TLS->IDPS). In Alert-only mode, only a copy is sent to IDPS engine.
solution: Use Alert-only mode for minimal performance impact if deny is not required. Review benchmarks at https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/firewall/firewall-performance. Use IDPS bypass list for known-good high-throughput traffic.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Firewall/Features & Functions/IDPS (IDS + IPS) Feature - Azure Firewall

symptom: Customer configures IDPS bypass list expecting performance improvement but sees no significant change in throughput or latency.
rootCause: IDPS bypass only bypasses signature coverage (no alert/deny action), but packets still pass through the IDPS engine which is the performance bottleneck. It is NOT a full traffic bypass.
solution: No full bypass option currently available. Acknowledge this limitation. Consider Alert-only mode or network architecture changes to avoid IDPS for performance-sensitive flows.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Firewall/Features & Functions/IDPS (IDS + IPS) Feature - Azure Firewall

symptom: After IDPS signature update push, legitimate traffic is blocked/denied. Firewall logs show signature match like WEB_SPECIFIC_APPS HTTP POST Request with Attempted Directory Traversal.
rootCause: New IDPS signatures from signature feed may produce false positives against legitimate application traffic patterns.
solution: 1) If confirmed false positive, disable the specific signature ID in IDPS Signature settings. 2) Create an IDPS bypass list entry to allow the traffic.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Firewall/Features & Functions/IDPS (IDS + IPS) Feature - Azure Firewall

symptom: Persistent connectivity issues when AKS nodes reuse source ports with same IP for connections to different destination IPs via Azure Firewall application rules. Dataplane logs show packets allowed but TCP connections fail.
rootCause: When same FW instance handles both flows and application rule is hit, traffic is DNATed to SGW (proxy). SGW cannot distinguish between connections with same source IP:port going to different destinations.
solution: 1) Introduce NAT Gateway into architecture. 2) Avoid reusing same source port/IP combination. 3) Use Network rules instead of Application rules (traffic bypasses SGW).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Firewall/Features & Functions/Persistent connectivity issues when AKS nodes resuse ports and application rules are utilized

symptom: Azure Firewall with forced tunneling enabled cannot be started after being stopped/deallocated. Attempting to start results in HTTP 400 Bad Request error
rootCause: Stop/start operation is not supported for Azure Firewalls with forced tunneling enabled. The management IP configuration prevents normal reallocation
solution: Before stopping a forced-tunneling firewall, save the full firewall config (REST API output). After stop, the firewall must be deleted and recreated from scratch using the saved config. Alternatively, avoid stopping firewalls with forced tunneling enabled
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Firewall/Features & Functions/Stop & Start Azure Firewall

symptom: After deallocating (stopping) Azure Firewall, customer cannot restart it because ipConfigurations (PIPs, VNet, subnet) are wiped from ARM and no longer visible
rootCause: Azure Firewall Deallocate() removes all ipConfigurations from the ARM resource. The PIPs, VNet, and subnet info are not retained
solution: Before stopping, run the provided PowerShell script that captures all PIP names, VNet name, resource groups, and generates a ready-to-use restart script. The script handles single and multiple PIP scenarios (up to 250 PIPs). Save the output before executing Deallocate()
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Firewall/Features & Functions/Stop & Start Azure Firewall

symptom: Azure Firewall private IP address changes after stop and restart. Customer expects the same private IP but gets a different one
rootCause: Private IPs are assigned dynamically from the AzureFirewallSubnet during allocation. No guarantee the same IP will be assigned after restart
solution: Inform customer that private IP dynamic assignment is by design. If dependent resources reference the firewall private IP (e.g. UDRs), they must be updated after restart. Consider using Azure Firewall FQDN or DNS name instead of IP where possible
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Firewall/Features & Functions/Stop & Start Azure Firewall

symptom: Azure Firewall TLS inspection fails. Customer uploaded certificate to Key Vault but TLS inspection does not work or certificate validation fails
rootCause: Customer certificate does not meet the minimum CA path length requirement of 1. Azure Firewall generates a short-lived intermediate CA from the customer cert (requiring path length >= 1), which then issues the proxied server certificate (path length 0). This design cannot be changed
solution: Ensure the customer certificate in Key Vault is an Intermediate CA with CA path length >= 1, not a leaf certificate or a CA with path length 0. The private key must be exportable. Refer to Azure Firewall Premium certificates documentation for exact requirements
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Firewall/Features & Functions/TLS Inspection - Certificates

symptom: Azure Firewall TLS inspection returns HTTP custom error code 465 (TLSVersionMismatch) when inspecting traffic to destination server
rootCause: Destination server does not support TLS 1.2 or higher. Azure Firewall enforces at least TLS 1.2 for all secure connections during TLS inspection
solution: Upgrade the destination server to support TLS 1.2 or higher. If the server cannot be upgraded, create a network rule bypass for that specific destination to skip TLS inspection. Check AzureFirewallApplicationRule logs for the 465 error code
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Firewall/Features & Functions/TLS Inspection - Certificates

symptom: TLS inspection is not occurring on Azure Firewall even though it is configured. Traffic passes through without being inspected or decrypted
rootCause: Traffic is matching a network rule instead of an application rule. TLS inspection only works with application rules that have the TLS inspection checkbox enabled. Additionally, certain web categories with PII content have TLS inspection disabled by design
solution: Ensure traffic matches an application rule with TLS inspection enabled. Verify rule processing order - network rules are evaluated before application rules. If using web categories, check the MSFT JSON file for the category to confirm TLS inspection is allowed. For specific URLs in PII categories, create explicit application rules with TLS enabled
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Firewall/Features & Functions/TLS Inspection - Certificates

symptom: Unable to reach Azure External Load Balancer or VM Public IP through Azure Firewall DNAT rule after attaching a NAT Gateway to the Azure Firewall subnet. DNAT rule shows traffic in logs but requests never reach the backend VM
rootCause: NAT Gateway takes priority over Azure Firewall for SNAT. When NAT GW is attached to the firewall subnet, outbound DNAT traffic is SNATd using the NAT GW public IP instead of the Azure Firewall public IP, breaking the return path routing
solution: Update the backend resource (AKS/VM) subnet UDR: replace Azure Firewall Public IP route with NAT Gateway Public IP -> Next hop Internet. Update NSG on backend subnet to allow inbound traffic from NAT Gateway Public IP. Keep 0.0.0.0/0 -> Azure Firewall Private IP route unchanged
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Firewall/How To/Accessing an Azure Public Load Balancer through Azure Firewall when a NAT Gateway is attached to the Azure Firewall subnet

symptom: Azure Firewall enters Failed provisioning state. Custom DNS servers are configured in the Firewall Policy but the firewall becomes unresponsive
rootCause: Customer configured the Azure Firewall own private IP as a custom DNS server in the Firewall Policy. Using its own IP as custom DNS creates a circular dependency causing the failed state
solution: Remove the Azure Firewall private IP from the custom DNS server list in the Firewall Policy. Use Azure default DNS (168.63.129.16) or external DNS servers reachable without routing through the firewall. Ensure custom DNS servers in the policy match the VNet DNS configuration. Check GatewayManagerLogsTable for errors
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Firewall/How To/Azure Firewall with Spring Cloud

symptom: Azure Spring Cloud/Apps connectivity fails through Azure Firewall despite firewall logs showing traffic as allowed. Spring Cloud PG detects route for Azure Firewall subnet with next hop VPNGateway
rootCause: ExpressRoute circuit in a peered VNet propagates a default route (0.0.0.0/0) to all peered VNets including the Azure Firewall subnet. This causes the firewall outbound traffic to route to on-premises via ExpressRoute, creating asymmetric routing
solution: Add a UDR on the Azure Firewall subnet with 0.0.0.0/0 -> Next hop Internet to override the ExpressRoute-propagated default route. Ensure Spring Cloud subnets have UDR pointing to the Azure Firewall private IP. Verify with test traffic on firewall instances
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Firewall/How To/Azure Firewall with Spring Cloud

symptom: DNS resolution failures and timeouts through Azure Firewall DNS proxy. AzureFirewallDnsProxy logs show errors like 'read udp FW-IP:port->DNS-IP:53: i/o timeout' for domains like mcr.microsoft.com, crl.microsoft.com
rootCause: Custom DNS servers configured in Azure Firewall Policy are not responding to DNS queries forwarded by the firewall DNS proxy. The DNS servers may be unreachable, overloaded, or improperly configured
solution: Switch to Azure default DNS (168.63.129.16) if possible. If custom DNS is required, ensure DNS servers are reachable from the Azure Firewall. Run packet captures on the DNS servers to verify they receive and respond to queries. Check AzureFirewallDnsProxy diagnostic logs in Log Analytics
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Firewall/How To/Azure Firewall with Spring Cloud

symptom: Internal RFC 1918 private IP traffic (e.g. 192.168.x.x to 172.16.x.x) is being SNATd to Azure Firewall instance IP instead of preserving the original source IP. Packet captures on destination show firewall instance IP as source
rootCause: Customer removed IANAPrivateRanges from the Azure Firewall Private IP Traffic Prefixes/SNAT configuration. By default Azure Firewall does not SNAT RFC 1918 traffic, but removing IANAPrivateRanges causes all traffic to be SNATd
solution: Re-add IANAPrivateRanges to the Private Traffic Prefixes in Azure Firewall settings (Portal > Firewall > Private IP ranges). For public IP ranges used internally (VPN space), add those specific ranges. Use 0.0.0.0/0 to never SNAT, or 255.255.255.255/32 to always SNAT
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Firewall/Features & Functions/SNAT and NO SNAT Behavior
